coredata.dc:title: Interaction of photoswitchable nanoparticles with cellulosic materials for anticounterfeiting and authentication security documents

Roghani-Mamaqani, Hossein

coredata.dc:description: Invisible high-security anticounterfeiting inks were developed by surface-modification of functionalized latex nanoparticles with spiropyran derivatives, which display both photochromism and fluorescence upon UV irradiation at the same time. The functionalized latex nanoparticles show different spherical, doughnut-like, and red blood cell-like morphologies depending on concentration and type of its functional groups. Microscopic investigation of the impregnated cellulosic papers with latex nanoparticles display that epoxy-functionalized spherical latex nanoparticles have considerable packing and stability on the surface of the cellulosic substrate by formation of hydrogen bonding. Epoxy-functionalized latex nanoparticles containing spiropyran were used for preparation of high-security photochromic/fluorescent anticounterfeiting inks for print-marking of different cellulosic security documents such as certificate, money, and passport with different marks. The printed marks and finger-prints on the cellulosic security documents display photochromism and red fluorescence emission upon UV irradiation with high intensity and brightness.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the preparation of functional latex nanoparticles by emulsifier-free emulsion polymerization and physical or chemical incorporation of SPOH and SPCOOH on the surface of the latex nanoparticles.
DLS analysis for the functionalized latex nanoparticles before and after modification with SPOH and SPCOOH: (A) CLNPs, (B) ELNPs, and (C) HLNPs series.
SEM images for the functionalized latex nanoparticles with different functional comonomers: (A-Aʺ) CLNPs-10, (B-Bʺ) CLNPs-20, (C-Cʺ) ELNPs-10, (D-Dʺ) ELNPs-20, (E-Eʺ) HLNPs-10, and (F-Fʺ) HLNPs-20.
TEM images for the functionalized latex nanoparticles with different functional comonomers: (A-Aʺ) CLNPs-20, (B-Bʺ) ELNPs-20, and (C-Cʺ) HLNPs-20.

Photochromic color changes and fluorescence emissions of the spiropyran-modified latex nanoparticles (with a solid content of about 0.1 wt%) under UV and visible light irradiations. The name of samples is respectively from left to right: ELNPs-20-SPOH, ELNPs-10-SPNH, CLNPs-10-SPOH, HLNPs-20-SPCOOH, CLNPs-20-SPOH, HLNPs-10-SPCOOH, ELNPs-20-SPNH, CLNPs-20-SPNH, CLNPs-10-SPNH, and ELNPs-10-SPOH.

UV–vis spectra of the spiropyran-modified latex nanoparticles (with a solid content of about 0.1 wt%) before and after UV illumination (365 nm): (A) CLNPs-10-SPOH, (B) CLNPs-20-SPOH, (C) CLNPs-10-SPNH, (D) CLNPs-20-SPNH, (E) ELNPs-10-SPOH, (F) ELNPs-20-SPOH, (G) ELNPs-10-SPNH, (H) ELNPs-20-SPNH, (I) HLNPs-10-SPCOOH, and (J) HLNPs-20-SPCOOH.

SEM images of the impregnated cellulosic paper and tissue with spiropyran-modified latex nanoparticles: (A and Aʹ) Neat cellulosic paper, (B and Bʹ) neat tissue, (C-Cʺʹ) impregnated cellulosic paper with CLNPs-20-SPOH, (D-Dʺʹ) impregnated tissue with CLNPs-20-SPOH, (E-Eʺʹ) impregnated cellulosic paper with ELNPs-20-SPOH, and (F-Fʺʹ) impregnated cellulosic paper with HLNPs-20-SCOOH.
Application of spiropyran-modified latex nanoparticles (the ELNPs-20-SPOH sample) as the anticounterfeiting and rewritable ink for print-marking of the security documents.
Security print-marking on money and passport by using of different stumps loaded with photochromic and fluorescent anticounterfeiting ink based on ELNPs-20-SPOH.
Coating of the spiropyran-modified latex nanoparticles (the ELNPs-20-SPOH sample) on cellulosic papers and investigation of the rewritable photopatterns.

head.ce:title: Interaction of photoswitchable nanoparticles with cellulosic materials for anticounterfeiting and authentication security documents

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Invisible high-security anticounterfeiting inks were prepared by surface-modification of functionalized latex nanoparticles with spiropyran derivatives, which display both photochromism (coloration to purple) and fluorescence emission (highly intense and luminous red color) upon UV irradiation at the same time.

Invisible high-security anticounterfeiting inks were developed by surface-modification of functionalized latex nanoparticles with spiropyran derivatives, which display both photochromism and fluorescence upon UV irradiation at the same time. The functionalized latex nanoparticles show different spherical, doughnut-like, and red blood cell-like morphologies depending on concentration and type of its functional groups. Microscopic investigation of the impregnated cellulosic papers with latex nanoparticles display that epoxy-functionalized spherical latex nanoparticles have considerable packing and stability on the surface of the cellulosic substrate by formation of hydrogen bonding. Epoxy-functionalized latex nanoparticles containing spiropyran were used for preparation of high-security photochromic/fluorescent anticounterfeiting inks for print-marking of different cellulosic security documents such as certificate, money, and passport with different marks. The printed marks and finger-prints on the cellulosic security documents display photochromism and red fluorescence emission upon UV irradiation with high intensity and brightness.

ce:section.ce:para: Counterfeiting of security documents has become an important global challenge for companies, governments, and customers. (Abdollahi, Sahandi-Zangabad, & Roghani-Mamaqani, 2018; Andres, Hersch, Moser, & Chauvin, 2014; Bao et al., 2015; Jiang et al., 2016; Li, Wang, Zhang, & Yu, 2019; Shi et al., 2019) Using of novel anticounterfeiting materials is a key point and solution for this problem, because update of security marks in documents can remarkably reduce the copy and counterfeiting of brands and documents such as bank notes, diplomas, certificates, money, passport, and national card. (Abdollahi et al., 2018b; Abdollahi, Roghani-Mamaqani, & Razavi, 2019; Andres et al., 2014; Huang et al., 2017; Wang, Tian, Zhang, Yang, & Yin, 2018) Photoluminescent and fluorescent compounds are the most significant anticounterfeiting materials extensively studied because of their ability to display emissions with different colors under UV irradiation. (Kang, Lee, & Nam, 2018; Qi et al., 2017; Tsai, Lai, Tseng, Liao, & Chan, 2017) These materials can be classified as inorganic paints, organic dyes, rare earth complexes, polymer dots, polymer particles, and supramolecular categories. Polymeric anticounterfeiting materials have received a great deal of attention due to their potential for increasing life time, photofatigue resistance, and fluorescence emission intensity of photoluminescence compounds in security documents after exposure to UV light for several times in a long period of time. Photoluminescent and fluorescent compounds can chemically or physically be incorporated into the polymer particles using various methods, such as self-assembly, nanoprecipitation, emulsion polymerization, and precipitation polymerization. (Abdollahi et al., 2018b; Abousalman-Rezvani, Eskandari, Roghani-Mamaqani, & Salami-Kalajahi, 2019; Abousalman-Rezvani, Eskandari, Roghani-Mamaqani, Mardani, & Salami-Kalajahi, 2019; Hajebi, Abdollahi, Roghani-Mamaqani, & Salami-Kalajahi, 2019; Tsai et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2018; Yildiz et al., 2011; Zhu et al., 2006) In addition, size of the polymer particles should be controlled in the range of 200−500 nm for their applications in inkjet printers and also stability on the paper surface. The particles with larger size may cause accumulation at the head of printer nozzle because of their aggregation. However, diffusion of the particles with smaller size to the bulk of paper significantly decreases the fluorescence emission intensity. Such polymer particles can be prepared by emulsifier-free emulsion polymerization, which can be resulted in latex particles with spherical morphology and different functionalities. (Abdollahi, Mahdavian, & Salehi-Mobarakeh, 2015; Abdollahi, Sahandi-Zangabad, & Roghani-Mamaqani, 2018; Abdollahi et al., 2018b)

The most commonly used fluorescent compounds in development of anticounterfeiting polymer inks are organic dyes, polymer dots, quantum dots, semi-conductive polymers, upconversion nanoparticles, rare earth complexes, and fluorescent nanocrystals. (Beaujean et al., 2016; Díaz, Giordano, Azcárate, Jovin, & Jares-Erijman, 2013; Huang et al., 2019; Kumar, Singh, & Gupta, 2016; Liu, Zhou, Li, Deng, & Zhang, 2017; Martínez et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2006; Yang, Meir, Raanan, & Oron, 2019) Such anticounterfeiting materials display fluorescence emission with different red, green, orange, blue, white, and yellow colors under UV irradiation. A brief review of the recent studies displays that a wide range of fluorescent inks with similar properties such as excitation wavelength, color, and fluorescence emission were developed for anticounterfeiting applications. Such similarities may lead to decrease of security because fluorescence emission is a general security mark. Therefore, application of other materials with the ability of simultaneous exhibiting more than one security mark upon UV light is a very interesting and novel approach in development of anticounterfeiting inks. (Kumar et al., 2016; Tsai et al., 2017; Yoon et al., 2013) Photochromic compounds can be an interesting candidate for this approach because of its abilities for displaying color change (photochromism) and fluorescence emission simultaneously upon UV irradiation. Spiropyran, spiroxazine, diarylethene, fulgide, oxazolidine, azobenzene, and oxazine are the most commonly used photochromic compounds. (Abdollahi, Roghani-Mamaqani, Razavi, 2019; Abdollahi, Roghani-Mamaqani, Razavi, & Salami-Kalajahi, 2019; Fan et al., 2019; Jang et al., 2012; Qin, Sheng, & Zhang, 2018; Sheng et al., 2014) Different photochromism and fluorescence emission can be observed depending on the chemical structure and substituted functional groups (electron donor or electron withdrawing) of the photochromic compounds. (Abdollahi et al., 2015; Abdollahi, Rad, & Mahdavian, 2016; Stellacci et al., 1999) Spiropyran is among the most important photochromic compounds reversibly isomerized between the ring-closed/colorless/non-fluorescent/non-polar spiro form (SP) and ring-opened/colored/fluorescent/polar zwitterionic merocyanine (MC) structure upon UV (SP to MC) and visible light irradiation (MC to SP). Spiropyran displays different stimuli-chromic behavior such as photochromism, (Chen et al., 2014; Klajn, 2014; Moo, Presolski, & Pumera, 2016) mechanochromism, (Li, Zhang, Zhou, & Zhu, 2018) ionochromism, (Baldrighi, Locatelli, Desper, Aakeröy, & Giordani, 2016) electrochromism, (Minkin, 2004) acidochromism, (Genovese et al., 2017) piezochromism, (Liu, Wang, Wang, Munyentwari, & Zhou, 2015) solvatochromism, (Abdollahi, Alinejad, & Mahdavian, 2017) and hydrochromism (Qin et al., 2018) depending on its chemical structure. This compound has extensively been used for the preparation of photochromic polymers and nanoparticles by its chemical or physical incorporation into different polymer matrices. Photochromism and fluorescence emission of spiropyran can significantly be affected by polarity of the surrounding media and polymer matrix, which is because of physical interactions of the MC form with polar chemicals. In development of anticounterfeiting inks, spiropyran can offer some advantages such as dual photochromic and fluorescent security marks, different colors depending to the polymer matrix, and invisible or rewritable prints and photopatterns. Such anticounterfeiting polymer inks show both the photochromic color change and fluorescence emission respectively after and under UV illumination, which can significantly increase security of documents in comparison with the previous fluorescent inks. In addition, chemical or physical incorporation of spiropyran to the hydrophobic polymer nanoparticles results in remarkable increase of photofatigue resistance, reversibility, and photochromic properties and also reduction of negative photochromism. Latex nanoparticles with different functional groups can be synthesized by using various functional monomers for their stabilization on the paper surface by polar-polar or hydrogen bonding interactions, where the polarity of functional groups may result in different color changes and fluorescence emission of spiropyran upon UV irradiation. (Abdollahi et al., 2018a; Abdollahi et al., 2017, 2018b)

Anticounterfeiting inks have extensively been studied in security marking applications, but a few numbers of studies have focused on polymer nanoparticles containing photochromic compounds. Chan and coworkers prepared polymeric anticounterfeiting inks based on semiconducting polymer dots and spiropyran-functionalized poly (styrene-co-maleic anhydride). Nanoprecipitation of their solution in aqueous media resulted in the formation of a colloidal solution containing fluorescent and photochromic polymer particles with particle size in the range of 10−30 nm. (Tsai et al., 2017) The prepared inks were used for handwriting by a pen as well as inkjet printing on the cellulosic papers and polymeric substrates, to put security marks on documents. Small size of polymer particles and their diffusion into the bulk of paper which remarkably reduced the fluorescence intensity and also use of semiconducting polymers which increased the final price of the ink can be counted as the two important challenges. Abdollahi and coworkers prepared a new category of anticounterfeiting inks based on spiropyran-containing latex nanoparticles by emulsifier-free emulsion polymerization, where the latex nanoparticles were functionalized by using methacrylic acid (MAA), hydroxyethyl methacrylate (HEMA), and glycidyl methacrylate (GMA). (Abdollahi et al., 2018a, 2018b) The functionalized latex nanoparticles with size in the range of 400−900 nm were used for handwriting and spraying on cellulosic papers with the aid of security marking and photopatterning applications. Stabilization of the particles on the paper surface was achieved by considering their large particle size and also polar functional groups on the particles surface, which finally led to increase of photochromic and fluorescence characteristics. A significant challenge was using of the anticounterfeiting ink for inkjet printing or stamping on cellulosic papers. In addition, due to the chemical-incorporation of spiropyran into the particles bulk, coloration and fluorescence emission intensity were medium for this ink. This problem can be solved by post-polymerization modification of functionalized latex nanoparticles with spiropyran derivatives for their physical or chemical incorporation on the particles surface. By this method, concentration of spiropyran on the particles surface can remarkably be increased, which can finally result in high intensity coloration and fluorescence emission.

In this study, functionalized latex nanoparticles with different morphologies were prepared by semi-continuous emulsifier-free emulsion polymerization of styrene in the presence of divinylbenzene (DVB) as crosslinker and MAA, HEMA, and GMA as the functional comonomers. Size and morphology investigation of the functionalized latex nanoparticles displayed that these particles have different particle size and morphologies depending to the amount and type of the functional monomers. Invisible anticounterfeiting photochromic/fluorescent inks were prepared by post-polymerization modification of the prepared latex nanoparticles with hydroxylated spiropyran (SPOH) and carboxylated spiropyran (SPCOOH) derivatives, where both of them were chemically and physically incorporated at the surface of the latex nanoparticles. Photochromic properties, fluorescence emission, kinetics of photochromism (SP↔MC isomerization), and photofatigue resistant characteristics were studied by UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopies. As an advanced application, invisible photochromic/fluorescent anticounterfeiting inks were prepared from spiropyran-containing latex nanoparticles by formulation with polyethylene glycol, ethanol, and glycerol. The formulated ink was used for loading to different stamps and correspondingly print-marking of different security documents such as passport, certificate, and money to induce a high security photochromic/fluorescent marks. Also, application of latex nanoparticles for photopatterning was studied by spraying of the latex on cellulosic papers and then UV illumination under various masks. This is a novel strategy for development of high-security photochromic/fluorescent anticounterfeiting inks based on spiropyran containing latex nanoparticles for security print-marking and rewritable photopatterning.

2,3,3-Trimethylindolenin, 3-bromopropionic acid, 2-bromoethanol, and 2-hydroxy-5-nitrobenzaldehyde, used for synthesis of (R/S)-2-(3′,3′-dimethyl-6-nitro-3′H spiro[chromene-2,2′-indol]-1′-yl)ethanol (SPOH) and l-(β-carboxyethyl)-3′,3′-dimethyl-6-nitrospiro (indoline-2′,2[2H-1] benzopyran) (SPCOOH), ethylenediamine (ED), and glycidyl methacrylate (GMA) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Divinylbenzene (DVB), styrene, methacrylic acid (MAA), hydroxyethyl methacrylate (HEMA), polyethylene glycol (PEG, MW: 3500−4500 g/mol), glycerol, potassium persulfate (KPS), piperidine, SPAN 80, sodium hydrogen carbonate (NaHCO3), sodium chloride (NaCl), and all of the solvents were supplied by Merck Chemical Company. High quality filter paper (WHA10347512, Whatman qualitative filter paper for technical use, grade shark skin, diameter of 185 mm, Germany) and cotton tissue were used as substrates for deposition of the latex particles. Distilled-deionized (DI) water was used in all the recipes, and all of the materials were used without further purifications.

SPOH was prepared by a three-step synthesis procedure from 2,3,3-trimethylindolenin, 2-bromoethanol, and 2-hydroxy-5-nitrobenzaldehyde according to the procedure reported by Raymo.(Raymo, Giordani, White, & Williams, 2003) Also, SPCOOH was synthesized in three steps from 2,3,3-trimethylindolenin, 3-bromopropionic acid, and 2-hydroxy-5-nitrobenzaldehyde according to the literature.(Doddi, Ramakrishna, Venkatesh, & Bangal, 2015; Tian & Tian, 2014) Chemical structure of SPOH and SPCOOH were characterized by 1H NMR spectroscopy in DMSO, and the resulted spectra are presented in Figs. S1 and S2 (Supporting Information).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the functionalized latex nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesis of the functionalized latex nanoparticles with different morphologies and a solid content of about 10 wt% was carried out by a typical semi-continuous emulsifier-free emulsion polymerization procedure using the literature,(Abdollahi et al., 2018a, 2018b) as schematically shown in Fig. 1 . According to the procedure presented in Table S1 (Supporting Information), the required amounts of KPS, NaCl, and NaHCO3 were dissolved in DI water (90 mL) and transferred into the reactor under continuous flow of nitrogen gas, and then styrene (10 g) and DVB (0.1 g) were slowly added to the mixture under stirring at room temperature. In the next step, the reactor temperature was increased to 80 °C for initiation of the polymerization reaction under reflux. In order to preparation of the functionalized latex nanoparticles, the functional monomers such as MAA, HEMA, and GMA (10 and 20 wt% with respect to the styrene monomer) were added into the reaction mixture 3 h after the initiation of polymerization reaction, and then the reaction was continued for 3 h to form the carboxylated latex nanoparticles (CLNPs), hydroxylated latex nanoparticles (HLNPs), and also epoxidized latex nanoparticles (ELNPs), respectively. According to Table S1 (Supporting Information), an appropriate amount of SPAN 80 was added to the prepared latex samples and stirring was continued for 24 h for full incorporation of the nonionic surfactant on surface of the latex nanoparticles. The total coagulation and conversion were less than 1 wt% and more than 95%, respectively, measured by the gravimetric method presented in the Section 3 of Supporting Information. The final latex nanoparticle samples were obtained after three cycles of centrifugation in 7500 rpm for 20 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface-modification of the functionalized latex nanoparticles with SPOH and SPCOOH
ce:section.ce:para: For preparation of the photochromic and fluorescent latex nanoparticles, SPOH and SPCOOH were physically and chemically incorporated on the surface of the functionalized latex nanoparticles via a post polymerization modification method reported by Diamond and Sun. (Scarmagnani et al., 2008; Sun et al., 2013) By a typical procedure, the CLNPs and ELNPs latexes were functionalized with amine groups by their surface-modification according to the methods reported by Diamond and Yang.(Muzammil, Khan, & Stuparu, 2017; Scarmagnani et al., 2008; Song, Liu, Yang, & Bai, 2015) A solution of SPCOOH (0.03 g, or 3 wt% with respect to the amount of polymer in functional latexes with solid content of 10 wt%) in ethanol (1 mL) was slowly added to 10 mL of the latex samples in 30 min, and the mixture was stirred at ambient temperature and dark space over night to complete chemical attachment of SPCOOH to latex nanoparticles by an amidation reaction. Finally, the modified latex samples were purified after three cycles of centrifugation in 7500 rpm for 15 min and washing with fresh DI water for several times to obtain CLNPs-10-SPNH, CLNPs-20-SPNH, ELNPs-10-SPNH, and ELNPs-20-SPNH latex samples. In the samples names, 10 and 20 denote the functional monomer (MAA, HEMA, and GMA) weight ratio with respect to the styrene monomer. In a similar procedure, SPOH (0.03 g or 3 wt%, a solution in 1 mL ethanol) was physically attached to CLNPs and ELNPs latex samples to prepare CLNPs-10-SPOH, CLNPs-20-SPOH, ELNPs-10-SPOH and ELNPs-20-SPOH latex samples. These samples were prepared and purified by the same methods mentioned for chemical attachment of SPCOOH to the latex nanoparticles. In addition, SPCOOH (0.03 g or 3 wt%, a solution in 1 mL ethanol) was physically attached to HLNPs latex samples according to the above mentioned procedure for preparation of the ELNPs-10-SPOH and ELNPs-20-SPOH samples. Physical attachment of SPOH and SPCOOH chromophores on the latex nanoparticles was carried out by the formation of strong hydrogen bonding between the hydroxyl and carboxylic acid functional groups of the chromophores with hydroxyl, carboxylic acid, and epoxy functional groups of the latex nanoparticles. Finally, the spiropyran molecules were stabilized on the surface of the latex nanoparticles. Total amounts of the chemically or physically incorporated SPOH and SPCOOH chromophores to the functional latex nanoparticles were determined by coagulation of the latexes, measurement of UV absorbance of the remaining serum by UV–vis analysis, and converting the absorbance data to the concentration by using calibration curves of the SPOH and SPCOOH standard solutions. It was found that more than 95% of the added SPOH and SPCOOH were physically and chemically incorporated in these samples. The final photochromic and photoluminescent latex samples were obtained after three cycles of centrifugation in 8500 rpm for 15 min and dispersion in fresh DI water to obtain latex samples with a solid content of 10 wt%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the invisible anticounterfeiting inks
ce:section.ce:para: Preparation of the invisible photochromic and fluorescent anticounterfeiting inks was carried out by mixing of a solution of PEG (0.5 g, MW about 4000 g/mol) and glycerol (0.5 g) in 4 mL ethanol with 10 mL of latex samples (CLNPs-10-SPNH, CLNPs-20-SPNH, ELNPs-10-SPNH, ELNPs-20-SPNH, CLNPs-10-SPOH, CLNPs-20-SPOH, ELNPs-10-SPOH, ELNPs-20-SPOH, HLNPs-10-SPCOOH, and HLNPs-20-SPCOOH) at ambient conditions under stirring for 24 h. PEG and glycerol were added for increase of viscosity and colloidal stability of the inks, and also ethanol was added for decrease of drying time after printing on the paper. After completion of the required mixing time, the homogenous colloidal inks can be used for writhing by pen, finger-printing, and print-marking by stump for labeling of security documents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: High-security anticounterfeiting print-marking and rewritable photo-patterning
ce:section.ce:para: The anticounterfeiting inks were directly used without any additives for charging of the designed stumps, and then applied on the certificate, money, and passport to induce the high-security anticounterfeiting print-marks which have both of the photochromism and fluorescence emission characteristics. Also, finger-print marking of passport was carried out after impregnation of finger with anticounterfeiting ink. In addition, rewritable photo-patterns were prepared by spraying of the initial photochromic and fluorescent latex nanoparticles on the cellulosic papers and drying at 45 °C for 5 h. These papers were exposed to UV light irradiation (365 nm) for 5 min under various masks, and the patterns were investigated by photography before and after UV light illumination. Discoloration of patterns was carried out by visible light illumination and also heating at 40 °C for 5 min.

Characterization of the chemical structure of SPOH and SPCOOH was carried out by proton nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) spectroscopy using a Bruker DPX400 MHz apparatus in CDCl3 as solvent. Measurement of the particle size and its distribution was performed by using ZETASIZER NANO ZSP dynamic light scattering (DLS, Malvern, United Kingdom) at 25 °C. For this purpose, the initial latex nanoparticles were diluted in DI water with concentration of about 1 mg/mL. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) micrographs were taken by a Tescan Mira III (Czech Republic). A drop of diluted latex samples was placed on the sample holder and dried in vacuum at 25 °C. Then, it was put in vacuum, evacuated, and a layer of gold was deposited under flushing with argon by using EMITECH K450x sputter-coating (England). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken by using of LEO 906 instrument (Zeiss, Oberkochen, Germany) with an accelerating voltage of 100 kV to study morphology of the latex nanoparticles bearing different functional groups. For this purpose, a drop of samples (with concentration of about 1 mg/mL) was placed on the carbon-coated copper grid and dried in vacuum at 25 °C. Photochromic properties of the samples were investigated by UV–vis analysis by using Jenway 6705 UV/Visible Scanning Spectrophotometer (United Kingdom). Fluorescence emission of the samples was studied by using of JASCO FP-750 Spectrofluorometer (Japan). Diluted latex samples with a concentration of 1 mg/mL were used for UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy analysis. To evaluate photochromic properties, the excitation was done by a UV lamp (365 nm, 6 W/m2), CAMAG 12VDC/VAC (50/60 Hz, 14VA, Switzerland). Also, the source for visible light was a common LED lamp (8 W/ m2). The UV and visible irradiation times were set at 5 min for all the samples.

Photochromic compounds, which display photochromism after UV light irradiation and also fluorescence emission under UV illumination, have remarkably been used for development of anticounterfeiting polymer inks. (Abdollahi et al., 2018b; Huang et al., 2019; Qi et al., 2017; Tsai et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2018) Dual anticounterfeiting characteristics (fluorescence and photochromism) of such polymeric inks can help for increasing security of the printed papers and correspondingly decrease of counterfeiting of high-security documents. Spiropyran is among the most applicable photochromic compounds that can be used in preparation of high-security photochromic/fluorescence anticounterfeiting inks based on polymer latex nanoparticles. Acrylate spiropyran monomer can chemically be incorporated into the latex nanoparticles by copolymerization with other comonomers through heterogeneous polymerization methods such as emulsion, miniemulsion, and emulsifier-free emulsion polymerization. UV light excites the spiropyran molecules located on the surface of the latex nanoparticles and cannot diffuse to the particles. Presence of high concentration of spiropyran molecules into bulk of the latex nanoparticles in copolymerization methods significantly reduces efficiency of photochromism and fluorescence emission, which is counted as a deficiency for such methods. On the other hand, incorporation of high concentration of spiropyran to the latex nanoparticles can induce some disadvantages, such as coagulation and instability of the latex nanoparticles during polymerization process, broadening of the particle size distribution, increase of particle size, and dye aggregation.(Abdollahi et al., 2018a; Abdollahi et al., 2015, 2018b) Therefore, incorporation of spiropyran on the surface of the latex nanoparticles after polymerization process by physical or chemical methods can be a powerful solution for such issues. Surface-functionalization of latex nanoparticles with spiropyran derivatives results in increase of photochromic and fluorescence properties. Such a vision was used in the current study by chemical and physical attachment of the SPOH and SPCOOH chromophores on the surface of the functionalized latex nanoparticles by post polymerization modification methods, according to the literatures. (Muzammil et al., 2017; Scarmagnani et al., 2008; Song et al., 2015; Sun et al., 2013) As schematically shown in Fig. 1, functionalized latex nanoparticles were prepared by copolymerization of styrene and DVB via semi-continues emulsifier-free emulsion polymerization according to the procedure presented in Table S1 (Supporting Information). The subsequent feeding of the functional monomers (MAA, GMA, and HEMA) was carried out at 3 h after start of polymerization reaction to locate functional groups on the surface of particles. The latex samples were prepared with different functional groups such as carboxylic acid (CLNPs), epoxy (ELNPs), and hydroxyl (HLNPs) in 10 and 20 wt% with respect to the styrene monomer. In the second step, spiropyran derivatives (SPOH and SPCOOH) were chemically and physically incorporated to the surface of latex nanoparticles by post polymerization modification strategies. For chemical incorporation of the SPCOOH molecules on the surface of particles, CLNPs and ELNPs were modified with ED to form the amine-functional groups. Then, SPCOOH was incorporated at the surface by amidation reaction between the carboxylic acid groups of SPCOOH and amine functionalities of the ED-modified latex nanoparticles to obtain the CLNPs-10-SPNH, CLNPs-20-SPNH, ELNPs-10-SPNH, and ELNPs-20-SPNH samples. Also, SPOH and SPCOOH were physically incorporated to the surface of the CLNPs, ELNPs, and HLNPs samples by formation of strong hydrogen bonding between different functional groups of the spiropyran derivatives and surface of the latex nanoparticles, which resulted in formation of the CLNPs-10-SPOH, CLNPs-20-SPOH, ELNPs-10-SPOH, ELNPs-20-SPOH, HLNPs-10-SPCOOH, and HLNPs-20-SPCOOH samples. These samples simultaneously display both of the photochromic and fluorescence optical properties with high intensity, which have potential applications in development of novel high-security anticounterfeiting polymer inks.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Size and morphology of the functionalized latex nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 shows particle size and its distribution for all the latex samples before and after modification with SPOH and SPCOOH chromophores. Number-average particle size of CLNPs-10 and CLNPs-20 is about 340 and 180 nm, respectively. Particle size decreases about 160 nm by increasing MAA content, which results from the increase of surface carboxylic acid groups and the surface charge density (Fig. 2(A)). As expected for CLNPs-10-SPNH and CLNPs-20-SPNH, after chemical attachment of SPCOOH on the surface of the amine-functionalized latex nanoparticles, pH of the latex samples increased to 8 and 9 (Table S1, Supporting Information) and results in increase of surface charge and decrease of particle size by deprotonation of the carboxylic acid functional groups on the surface. Physical incorporation of SPOH on the surface of the CLNPs samples resulted in remarkable reduction of particle size due to increase of particle surface charge density by formation of hydrogen bonding between the carboxylic acid and hydroxyl functionalities. However, a slight particle size variation was observed for the ELNPs samples after chemical and physical attachment of SPOH and SPCOOH, which results from less polarity of epoxy groups compared to carboxylic acid functionalities (Fig. 2(B)). In addition, particle size is increased by increasing of GMA content in the ELNPs samples from 250 to 320 nm, respectively for the ELNPs-10 and ELNPs-20 samples. Increase of particle size results from high concentration of nonpolar epoxy functional groups on the surface of ELNPs-20 particles. Other samples showed similar particle size and PDI values after modification with SPOH and SPCOOH, which confirms incorporation of spiropyran on the particle’s surface without any secondary nucleation or aggregation of the particles. Functionalization of the latex nanoparticles with HEMA resulted in increase of their size (400 nm for HLNPs-10 and 1050 nm for HLNPs-20) because of partial swelling of the particles with water, as displayed in Fig. 2(C). Considerable decrease of particle size after physical incorporation of SPCOOH molecules on the surface of particles can be attributed to increase of hydrophobicity of the surface and coverage of the hydroxyl functional groups by SPCOOH molecules upon formation of hydrogen bonding between the hydroxyl and carboxylic acid functionalities. In summary, particle size investigation before and after chemical or physical attachment of SPOH and SPCOOH on the surface of latex nanoparticles displays that chromophores were efficiently attached to the surface of particles without any dye aggregation and secondary particle nucleation. This is mainly supported by the constant PDI and also unimodal particle size distribution for all of the samples before and after the post polymerization modification processes. This can be a strong confirmation for higher efficiency of the post polymerization modification method compared to the copolymerization methods for preparation of photochromic latex nanoparticles containing spiropyran moieties on the surface. According to the literature, (Gong & Wang, 2008; Guo, van Ravensteijn, & Kegel, 2019; Thomson, Rudin, & Lajoie, 1996; Yang, Ming, Hu, Lu, & Fu, 1998) type and amount of the functional monomer can influence the particle size, its distribution, and morphology of the latex nanoparticles, as observed for size and its distribution in DLS results. Therefore, morphological study of the functional latex nanoparticles should be investigated by SEM and TEM for all the samples to show their spherical or non-spherical particle morphologies.

Fig. 3 displays SEM images for the latex samples containing different functional groups to investigate their effect on the morphology of latex nanoparticles. All the samples show different morphologies due to different characteristics of their surface layers. The SEM images of CLNPs-10 (Figs. 3 (A-Aʺ)) display mostly spherical particles with a hollow cavity at the surface of some particles, which is well-known as doughnut-like morphology.(Iskandar, Gradon, & Okuyama, 2003; Ng, Tay, & Hong, 2016; Tian et al., 2017) Increase of the MAA content from 10 to 20 wt% in the CLNPs-20 sample resulted in more uniform and monodisperse spherical nanoparticles with a decrease in the particle size and its distribution, as shown in Figs. 3 (B-Bʺ). Presence of the hollow cavities on the surface of CLNPs-10 particles is due to the water-deswelling of the particles with carboxylic acid functional groups after drying of the SEM sample. Decrease of the particle size and its distribution for CLNPs-20 in comparison with the CLNPs-10 sample can be attributed to increase of surface carboxylic acid groups, which is resulted in increase of the surface charge density by deprotonation of the carboxylic acid functional groups. Functionalization of the polystyrene latex nanoparticles with GMA was led to different morphologies such as spherical particles with smooth surface, spherical particles with a hollow cavity in the center (doughnut-like particles in Fig. 3(Dʹ)), and also wrinkled particles with some dent cavities on the surface (Fig. 3(Dʺ)), as shown in Fig. 3(C-Cʺ) for ELNPs-10 and Fig. 3(D-Dʺ) for ELNPs-20. The SEM images of ELNPs-10 and ELNPs-20 samples display the similar morphologies, particle size, and its distribution, with an inconsiderable effect of the amount of functional monomers on these characteristics. Such different morphologies can be attributed to incompatibility of the styrene monomer with acrylate monomers. The functional latex nanoparticles prepared based on methyl methacrylate and the same functional monomers (MAA, GMA, and HEMA) by similar polymerization method display just spherical particles with a smooth surface.(Abdollahi et al., 2018a, 2018b) Surprisingly, a novel morphology was observed for the functionalized polystyrene latex nanoparticles bearing hydroxyl functional groups on their surface, which is significantly affected by the amount of hydroxyl functional groups incorporated to the particles. Fig. 3(E-Eʺ) and (F-Fʺ) display a red blood cell-like morphology(Doshi, Zahr, Bhaskar, Lahann, & Mitragotri, 2009) for the HLNPs samples, where a shrinkage was observed in the case of HLNPs-10 (Fig. 3(Eʺ)). According to the DLS results, increase of HEMA content from 10 to 20 wt% resulted in remarkable growth of the particle size from 400 to 1050 nm, respectively for the HLNPs-10 to HLNPs-20 samples. Therefore, HLNPs-10 as the smallest latex nanoparticle shows remarkable shrinking because of its higher surface area that functionalized with hydrophilic hydroxyl groups. HLNPs-20 as the larger latex nanoparticles displays lower shrinkage after water removal. These functional latex samples have the same red blood cell-like morphology in colloidal solution state, and undergone different particle surface shrinking after water deswelling during sample preparation for SEM analysis. There are some significant points about the interesting morphologies observed for the functionalized polystyrene latex nanoparticles. Morphology of the particles is significantly affected by polarity of the functional monomers. In addition, amount of the functional comonomers can tune the final morphology, particle size, and its distribution. It is necessary to investigate other effective parameters on the morphology of the functional polystyrene latex nanoparticles prepared by emulsifier-free emulsion polymerization.

For further morphology investigation of the functional latex nanoparticles, TEM images were taken from the CLNPs-20, ELNPs-20, and HLNPs-20 samples, as shown in Fig. 4 . The CLNPs-20 sample shows spherical functional latex nanoparticles with narrow size distribution, as shown in Fig. 4 (A-Aʺ). As previously discussed, carboxylic acid functional groups on the surface of particles act as stabilizer. Therefore, increase of its concentration results in decrease of particle size and reduction of particle size distribution. As shown in Fig. 4 (B-Bʺ), spherical and doughnut-like morphologies are clearly observable for the ELNPs-20 latex nanoparticles. Phase separation of the styrene and GMA monomers is the main reason for observation of this interesting morphology. Fig. 4 (C-Cʺ) illustrate red blood cell-like morphology for the HLNPs-20 sample, where surface of the particles shows roughness because of its shrinking by water deswelling. This morphology has not been reported for the latex nanoparticles composed of hydrophobic styrene and hydrophilic HEMA, while it can be used in drug or gen-delivery and also bioimaging applications. Morphology of the latex nanoparticles is very important due to their application in development of anticounterfeiting inks for high-security anticounterfeiting print-marking and rewritable photo-patterning on cellulosic papers. The morphology of particles can influence their photochromic and fluorescence characteristics and also stability of the particles on the cellulosic matrix. For example, the spherical particles with narrow size distribution show highly-intense photochromism and fluorescence emission due to their minimum light reflectance. In addition, packing of the spherical particles is higher than the other particles with different morphologies, which results in increase of the particles number on the paper surface. Therefore, application of anticounterfeiting ink results in increase of the security characteristics such as intensity of the color change and fluorescence emission upon UV irradiation. In continue, photochromic properties of the spiropyran-modified latex nanoparticles will be focused to develop an efficient anticounterfeiting system for security marking and printing on different documents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photochromic and fluorescence properties of spiropyran-modified latex nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Spiropyran as an interesting photochromic compound displays photochromic color change and fluorescence emission simultaneously in response to UV irradiation (365 nm) by reversible isomerization between the non-polar SP and high-polar zwitterionic MC forms. The photochromism and fluorescence are characterized by absorbance in the range of 400−700 nm after UV illumination and also observation of red emission under UV light and a fluorescence emission in the range of 500−700 nm.(Keyvan Rad, Mahdavian, Salehi-Mobarakeh, & Abdollahi, 2016) Because of polar nature of the MC form, photochromic properties and fluorescence emission of spiropyran can significantly be affected by polarity of the surrounding media, which is well-known as solvatochromism, acidochromism, or basochromism. (Abdollahi, Mouraki, Sharifian, & Mahdavian, 2018; Abdollahi et al., 2015, 2016, 2017) The MC form is known as a metastable isomer which is stabilized in three different forms of deprotonated MC in basic media (pH > 7), protonated MC (MCH) in acidic media (pH < 7), and quinoidal MC (MCQ) in non-polar media such as aprotic solvents. Photochromic properties and fluorescence emission for each of the isomers are different and detectable, which has extensively been used in development of polarity, ion, pH, and also gas sensors.(Abdollahi et al., 2017, 2016; Abdollahi, Mouraki et al., 2018; Genovese, Athanassiou, & Fragouli, 2015, 2017; Park, Heo, & Kim, 2011; Shao et al., 2005) Fig. 5 shows the photochromic behavior and fluorescence emission of the spiropyran-modified latex nanoparticles upon UV light irradiation. Different color changes and also various fluorescence emissions can be observed for each sample as a result of their different pH values (Table S1, Supporting Information). Mahdavian and coworkers recently reported photochromic papers for photosensing of pH, and displayed that spiropyran have specific responsivity to UV irradiation in natural (pH 7-8) and acidic (pH 1-7) media.(Abdollahi, Mouraki et al., 2018) Protonation of the MC form can promote the photochromism and isomerization of the SP to MC form; however, deprotonation induce quenching of the responsivity and photochromism. As shown in Table S1 (Supporting Information), four various types of latex samples with pH in the range of 8–9 (CLNPs-10-SPNH, CLNPs-20-SPNH, ELNPs-10SPNH, and ELNPs-20-SPNH) display different fluorescence emissions under UV light without any photochromism after UV irradiation (Fig. 5). The yellow-colored CLNPs-10-SPNH and CLNPs-20-SPNH latex samples display light-blue fluorescence emission under UV irradiation and show highly intense yellow color after UV irradiation. The white-colored ELNPs-10SPNH and ELNPs-20-SPNH latex samples show purple fluorescence emission under UV light and display any color change or photochromism after excitation with UV irradiation (Fig. 5). The other samples with a pH of bellow 7 (Table S1, Supporting Information) display standard photochromism and red fluorescence emission upon UV irradiation. The photochromism and fluorescence emission of the samples were respectively investigated by UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy in the following for clarification of such observations.

Fig. 6 displays UV–vis spectra for all the spiropyran-modified latex nanoparticles before and after excitation by UV light irradiation (365 nm). The latex nanoparticles containing physically-decorated SPOH and SPCOOH chromophores display suitable photochromism and responsivity after UV illumination in pH 4-7.5, as indicated by broad absorbance peaks in the wavelength of 450−750 nm for the CLNPs-10-SPOH, CLNPs-20-SPOH, ELNPs-10-SPOH, ELNPs-20-SPOH, HLNPs-10-SPCOOH, and HLNPs-20-SPCOOH samples, respectively in Fig. 6 (A), (B), (E), (F), (I), and (J). The ELNPs samples have the maximum absorbance intensity (hyperchromism) and color change as a result of lower polarity of GMA compared to MAA and HEMA. The highly polar carboxylic acid and hydroxyl functional groups can induce negative photochromism, and correspondingly decrease the photochromic properties and absorbance intensity (hypochromism). This is confirmed by appearance of higher absorbance intensities for the HLNPs samples in comparison with the CLNPs. The differences between λmax of the samples is assigned to interactions of the polar functional groups with the MC form and its effects on ground state energy gaps of the conjugated MC structure, which is known as hypsochromism (blue shift or decrease of λmax to short wavelength or large frequency) and bathochromism (red shift or increase of λmax to large wavelength or short frequency) phenomena.(Keum et al., 2007; Klajn, Stoddart, & Grzybowski, 2010; Klajn, 2014; Rosario, Gust, Hayes, Springer, & Garcia, 2003) It is a strong confirmation for efficient interactions between the MC isomer and polar functional groups, where polarity of the functional groups can be significantly affect the photochromic and fluorescence properties of the anticounterfeiting inks. Comparing Fig. 6 (I) and (J) displays a clear hypochromism (decrease of absorbance intensity) for the HLNPs-20-SPCOOH sample with respect to HLNPs-10-SPCOOH. This is due to higher negative photochromism in HLNPs-20-SPCOOH because of its higher concentration of HEMA and consequently higher stability of the MC form through its polar interactions with the hydroxyl functional groups. As previously discussed in Fig. 5, chemically modified latex samples with SPCOOH (CLNPs-10-SPNH, CLNPs-20-SPNH, ELNPs-10-SPNH, and ELNPs-20-SPNH) have not photochromic properties. They just display fluorescence emission in light-blue and purple, which is different from the other samples with red fluorescence. Investigation of photochromic properties for these samples (Fig. 6 (C), (D), (G) and (F)) displays a weak photochromism and reversibility. The ELNPs-10-SPNH and ELNPs-20-SPNH samples display a remarkable hypsochromism of λmax (513 and 499 nm, respectively) because of high polar interactions between their NH functionalities with the MC molecules and deprotonation of their phenolate oxygen. In summary, physically-modified latex nanoparticles with SPOH and SPCOOH show suitable photochromic properties and fluorescence emission, especially in the case of ELNPs-10-SPOH and ELNPs-20-SPOH samples. Chemically-modified latex samples with SPCOOH display different fluorescence emission without photochromism, which should be further investigated by fluorescence spectroscopy.

Spiropyran as a photochromic compound displays color change and fluorescence emission in response to UV illumination by isomerization of the SP to MC form. The MC isomer shows a red fluorescence with an emission peak in the range of 500−700 nm, which can be affected by polarity and pH of the medium similar to the photochromic behavior. Fluorescence spectra of the CLNPs-10-SPNH, CLNPs-20-SPNH, ELNPs-10-SPNH, and ELNPs-20-SPNH samples are respectively shown in Fig. S3 (A–D) (Supporting Information). These samples show a weak fluorescence emission in the wavelength range of 400−600 nm with different colors compared to the other samples. As shown in Fig. 5, these samples show different fluorescence emission color (the CLNPs samples show light blue and ELNPs samples display purple fluorescence emission) without photochromism, which can be attributed to the solvatochromic effect in different pHs and quenching of photochromism or negative photochromism in highly polar media. (Abdollahi et al., 2017; Abdollahi, Mouraki et al., 2018) High-security anticounterfeiting inks displaying both of the photochromism (purple coloration) and fluorescence emission (red luminescence) have been developed in the current study. Based on the results obtained from UV–vis and fluorescence spectra, the CLNPs-10-SPNH, CLNPs-20-SPNH, ELNPs-10-SPNH, and ELNPs-20-SPNH samples are not suitable for this purpose. Therefore, the CLNPs-10-SPOH, CLNPs-20-SPOH, ELNPs-10-SPOH, ELNPs-20-SPOH, HLNPs-10-SPCOOH, and HLNPs-20-SPCOOH samples are selected for further investigation of photochromism kinetics and photofatigue resistance for development of a highly efficient anticounterfeiting ink for security printing, marking, and photo-patterning.

Responsivity of the anticounterfeiting inks is a significant challenge which requires detailed investigations. After printing of an anticounterfeiting ink on a document, the security marks should display reversible photochromism or fast fluorescence emission upon UV illumination. Therefore, the SP↔MC isomerization kinetics for the samples showing both of photochromic and fluorescence properties (CLNPs-10-SPOH, CLNPs-20-SPOH, ELNPs-10-SPOH, ELNPs-20-SPOH, HLNPs-10-SPCOOH, and HLNPs-20-SPCOOH) should be studied. Kinetics of the SP to MC and MC to SP isomerizations was investigated by exposing the diluted latex samples (with a solid content of about 0.1 wt%) to UV irradiation (wavelength of 365 nm) and visible light at different irradiation time intervals (20 s) and also measurement of the absorbance at specific λmax for each of the samples by UV–vis spectroscopy (Fig. 6). Afterward, the absorbance values were normalized with respect to the absorbance of each sample before UV and visible light irradiation. The normalized results are plotted as a function of irradiation time to obtain kinetic curves for the SP↔MC isomerization reactions, as shown in Fig. S4 (Supporting Information). To extract the kinetic equations and kinetic parameters (kc and T1/2 ), Origin software (Professional 2017) was used for fitting the resulted data with suitable curves. For this purpose, the SP to MC isomerization kinetics was fitted by using the BoxLucas1 model, and also the MC to SP isomerization (back reaction) was fitted by using the ExpDec1 model, according to the Eqs. 1 and 2.

where, A and A0 are the constant values and kc1 and kc2 represent the rate constants for the SP to MC (Eq. 1) and MC to SP (Eq. 2) photoisomerization upon UV and visible light irradiation, respectively. The normalized absorbance values at time t and ∞ were extracted from Fig. S4 (Supporting Information). It should be noted that kc and T1/2 are significant parameters for monitoring photoresponsivity of the latex nanoparticles to UV and visible light irradiation. Similar to the results observed for photochromic behavior of the samples in Fig. 6, different kinetic curves and parameters were observed for each of the samples in Fig. S4 and Table S2 (Supporting Information), resulted from the effect of local polarity (surface functional groups) on stabilization of the MC form. Fig.S4 (Supporting Information) shows the kinetic curves for the SP to MC and MC to SP photoisomerization. The fitted equations with excellent R2 (0.97-0.99) and kinetic parameters (kc and T1/2) extracted from the curves are presented in Table S2 (Supporting Information). According to our recent studies,(Abdollahi et al., 2018a, 2018b) the kinetic parameters show significant dependency to the polarity of functional groups on the latex nanoparticles and also pH of the medium, as a result of interactions between the polar functional groups and medium with the SP or MC molecules. Increase of polarity increases rate of the SP to MC isomerization (maximum kc and minimum T1/2 ) and decreases rate of the MC to SP isomerization (minimum kc and maximum T1/2 ). Correspondingly, decrease of polarity led to slower SP to MC and fast MC to SP isomerizations. It is resulted from high stability of polar zwitterionic MC structure in polar media and high stability of the SP form in non-polar surrounding. The results obtained from Table S2 (Supporting Information) display that the HLNPs samples have faster responsivity for SP↔MC isomerization (maximum kc1 and kc2 and also minimum T1/2 for both of the SP to MC and MC to SP isomerization) upon UV and visible light irradiation. This is an ideal responsivity for spiropyran coloration and discoloration upon UV and visible light irradiation at a very high rate. The CLNPs samples bearing high polar carboxylic acid functionalities display weak responsivity (minimum kc1 and kc2 and also maximum T1/2 for both of the SP to MC and MC to SP isomerizations) as a result of high polar interactions between the carboxylic acid functional groups with MC molecules, which can induce negative photochromism to the latex nanoparticles as confirmed by lower intensities of the absorbance peak in Fig. 6. In the case of ELNPs, increase of the epoxy functional groups content in the ELNPs-20-SPOH sample (increase of polarity) resulted in remarkable increase of the SP↔MC isomerization rate (maximum kc1 and kc2 and also minimum T1/2 for both of the SP to MC and MC to SP isomerizations) compared to the ELNPs-10-SPOH sample, according to Table S2 (Supporting Information). Higher intensity of photochromism was observed for the ELNPs-20-SPOH sample (Fig. 6) in addition to the fast kinetics of SP↔MC isomerization (Fig. S4, Supporting Information), where its kc and T1/2 are much closed to the HLNPs samples (Table S2, Supporting Information). Higher intensity of photochromism and fluorescence emission and fast responsivity are the most significant factors in anticounterfeiting materials. In summary, the photochromic behavior of spiropyran in the latex samples was significantly affected by polarity of the surface functional groups, and negative photochromism can be observed for most of the polar functional groups such as carboxylic acid in the CLNPs samples. Therefore, it is questionable that which of the samples is suitable for the anticounterfeiting ink applications. The ELNPs-20-SPOH sample displays fast SP↔MC isomerization and also photochromic behavior with high intensity (Fig. 6) without negative photochromism. Thus, it can be an ideal system when having suitable photofatigue resistance and stability on the paper surface. Finally, the photofatigue resistance of these samples should be investigated in continue.

Photostability, reversibility, and photoswitchability under alternative UV/visible light irradiation cycles or in a long time period are the most significant challenges in development of the advanced materials based on photochromic compounds. Such properties should be investigated by photofatigue resistance analysis, in which the absorbance intensity was measured after each UV/visible irradiation cycle for several times. All the diluted latex samples (CLNPs-10-SPOH, CLNPs-20-SPOH, ELNPs-10-SPOH, ELNPs-20-SPOH, HLNPs-10-SPCOOH, and HLNPs-20-SPCOOH) with a solid content of about 0.1 wt% were exposed to UV or visible light irradiation for 5 min, and their absorbance in a specific λmax (568, 560, 572, 569, 581, and 567 nm, respectively) was measured by UV–vis spectroscopy in more than 20 cycles and plotted against cycle number to obtain the photofatigue resistance curves, as shown in Fig. S5 (Supporting Information). Results show that the photochromic properties of the latex samples are fully reversible in a long period of time or several UV/visible light irradiation cycles. Especially, the ELNPs-20-SPOH sample shows remarkable photochromism/fluorescence intensity and fast SP↔MC isomerization. This is a strong confirmation for photoswitchability of color change and fluorescence emission of spiropyran molecules in the latex nanoparticles bearing polar functional groups. Polymer particles are a significant tool for protection of spiropyran toward environmental degradations and especially negative photochromism in polar media. Suitable photoswitchability and photofatigue resistance display efficient role of the polymer particles in protection of spiropyran toward environmental degradations. For final selection of the best sample for preparation of anticounterfeiting inks, it is needed to investigate stability of the latex nanoparticles with different morphologies on the paper and tissue surfaces.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Incorporation of latex nanoparticles on cellulosic paper and tissue
ce:section.ce:para: Spiropyran-modified latex nanoparticles were used for development of anticounterfeiting inks for security printing and marking on cellulosic materials. The packing of particles and their stability on cellulosic substrates are the most significant parameters which are remarkably affected by morphology of the latex nanoparticles and type of the functional groups. SEM images were taken from the impregnated cellulosic papers with the CLNPs-20-SPOH, ELNPs-20-SPOH, and HLNPs-20-SPCOOH latex samples, as shown in Fig. 10(C-Cʺʹ, E-Eʺʹ, and F-Fʺʹ, respectively). In addition, a piece of cotton tissue was impregnated with CLNPS-20-SPOH and the corresponding SEM images were shown in Fig. 10(D-Dʹʺ). For recording SEM images, some pieces of cellulosic paper and cotton tissue were soaked in the latex solution for 1 min, dried at 40 °C for 1 h, and coated with gold layer. As shown in Fig. 10(A-Aʹ and B-Bʹ, respectively), the neat cellulosic paper and cotton tissue have smooth fibers without any additional coatings. After incorporation of the latex nanoparticles into the cellulosic papers we can see change of the surface morphology of the cellulose fibers. Fig. 10(C-Cʹʺ and D-Dʹʺ, respectively) display the surface of cellulose and tissue fibers after treatment with CLNPs-20-SPOH. The latex nanoparticles show interesting packing and high stability on the fibers because of their spherical morphology and also carboxylic acid functional groups on the particle’s surface. Functional groups facilitate stability of particles on the fiber’s surface by formation of hydrogen bonding between the hydroxyl functionalities of cellulose with carboxylic acid groups of the latex nanoparticles. Fig. 10(E-Eʺʹ) show the impregnated cellulosic paper with the ELNPs-20-SPOH latex nanoparticles with an interesting packing and suitable stability on the cellulose surface. This results from the spherical morphology and also presence of epoxy groups on the ELNPs-20-SPOH latex nanoparticles with the ability of hydrogen bonding with hydroxyl groups of cellulosic substrates. As shown in the Fig. 10(F-Fʺʹ) for the HLNPs-20-SPCOOH sample, good packing and stability of the latex nanoparticles are not shown on the cellulose matrix, which is due to their non-spherical morphology and also weak interactions between the hydroxyl groups of the nanoparticles and cellulose (Fig. 7 (Fʺʹ)). The UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy results respectively for the photochromic and fluorescence emission of the latex nanoparticles in combination with the SEM images of impregnated cellulosic papers show that the ELNPs-20-SPOH sample is the best candidate for development of anticounterfeiting inks because of the following reasons: (I) Inconsiderable negative photochromism of the ELNPs-20-SPOH sample compared to the CLNPs-20-SPOH sample, because of its higher absorbance intensity of photochromism observed in Fig. 6(F); (II) It’s high-intensity color change (photochromism) and fluorescence emission upon UV light; (III) It’s higher rates for the SP↔MC isomerization and high photoswitchability, reversibility, and photofatigue resistant characteristics; and (IV) higher packing and stability of the particles after incorporation to the cellulosic matrix. Therefore, the ELNPs-20-SPOH latex sample is selected for development of photochromic and fluorescence anticounterfeiting inks with application in high-security anticounterfeiting print-marking and rewritable photo-patterning on cellulosic papers in continue.

ce:section.ce:section-title: High-security anticounterfeiting print-marking and rewritable photo-patterning
ce:section.ce:para: Anticounterfeiting ink is among the most significant advanced applications of the photochromic and fluorescent latex nanoparticles bearing the spiropyran photochromophore. Here, the ELNPs-20-SPOH latex sample was used for the preparation of the photochromic and fluorescent anticounterfeiting ink with application in high-security print-marking and photopatterning. The anticounterfeiting ink prepared from the ELNPs-20-SPOH latex nanoparticles was loaded to the stumps with specific designed marks, such as quick response code (QR), barcode, some schemes related to the SP and MC structures, and some words (photochrom and spiropyran). All of the stumps were used for security print-marking of a model security document, as shown in Fig. 8 . The print-marked document has some marks which are invisible under white light and visible upon UV irradiation (365 nm), which can be detected by coloration of the marks from colorless to purple by isomerization of the SP to MC isomer. For instance, the QR and barcode marks can be personalized for a person or brand, and characterized by barcode reader instruments after irradiation of the document with UV light and visibility of the security QR and barcode. In addition, the red fluorescence emission can be observed for the printed marks under UV irradiation. Anticounterfeiting stump is a fast and facile tool for security-marking of different documents by using of photochromic and fluorescence latex samples as invisible high-security inks.

Money, passport, and national card are among the most significant security documents which apply extensive costs to governments for inhibiting their copy and counterfeiting. Development of high-security anticounterfeiting ink can be a powerful solution for solving the security documents counterfeiting. Therefore, anticounterfeiting stumps loaded with the ink based on ELNPs-20-SPOH was used for printing of different security marks on money and also passport. The photochromic and fluorescence characteristics of the printed marks were investigated under and after UV irradiation (365 nm), and the images are presented in Fig. 9
. The printed marks on the monies (QR) and passport (security, barcode, and finger print) display a highly intense and luminous red fluorescence emission under UV light, due to isomerization of SP to MC. In addition, they are invisible under white light and converted to the visible colored marks after UV irradiation. The developed anticounterfeiting ink was used for applying fingerprint on the passport, which is invisible (discolored) under ambient light and converted to a visible (colored) one after UV irradiation. In addition, the fingerprint mark is observed with a highly intense and luminous red fluorescence emission under UV light. The coloration and fluorescence emission of the printed marks are fully reversible upon UV/visible irradiation cycles for several times in a long period of time. High-security of the developed anticounterfeiting ink is due to its simultaneous photochromism and fluorescence emission security characteristics, which is very interesting and important for increasing of safety and reduction of counterfeiting in different security documents. Fast coloration and fluorescence emission, long life time, high photofatigue resistance, high stability on the cellulosic papers, reversibility, photoswitchability, and also the facile preparation method are some unique advantages of the developed photochromic and fluorescent anticounterfeiting ink.

Photopatterning is a significant application of photochromic polymers containing spiropyran photoswitches. Development of photochromic and fluorescent polymers for induction of photopatterns on different substrates has been an interesting challenge. Cellulosic paper is the most important substrate for application of photopatterns. Efficient coating of the cellulosic papers with photochromic polymers has been a problem in the preparation of photopatterns which can be dissolved by using of photochromic latex nanoparticles. The ELNPs-20-SPOH sample is photochromic and fluorescent latex containing spherical polymer particles with epoxy functional groups on the surface. These characteristics result in high stability and interesting packing of the latex nanoparticles on cellulosic papers, as shown in Fig. 7. The rewritable cellulosic photopatterns were prepared by spraying of the HLNPs-20-SPOH latex on the paper and drying at 45 °C. The papers were exposed to UV irradiation under different masks for 1 min, where the colored photopatterns were clearly observed after removing the mask, as shown in Fig. 10. Such high-resolution and erasable photopatterning is fully reversible under alternated UV and visible light irradiation cycles for several times. The rewritable photopatterns on the cellulosic papers are initially discolored, and coloration occurred just upon UV irradiation (365 nm). Using of spiropyran-containing functionalized latex nanoparticles is an efficient, fast, and facile method for preparation of photochromic and fluorescent photopatterns on cellulosic papers.

High-security photochromic and fluorescent anticounterfeiting inks based on spiropyran-modified latex nanoparticles was prepared by an efficient, fast, facile, and scalable strategy. The functionalized latex nanoparticles were prepared by semi-continues emulsifier-free emulsion polymerization and then post-polymerization modification of the nanoparticles with SPOH and SPCOOH chromophores by the physical and chemical methods. DLS, SEM, and TEM results display that morphology and size of the latex nanoparticles were significantly affected by the type and concentration of the functional groups. Latex nanoparticles with spherical, doughnut-like, and red blood cell-like morphologies were prepared for the first time, depending on the type and amount of functional groups. Polarity of the functional groups can affect the photochromic properties and fluorescence emission of the latex samples, where the CLNPs and HLNPs latex samples display negative photochromism. On the other hand, the ELNPs latex samples show interesting photochromic and fluorescence emission intensity, fast responsivity toward UV and visible light irradiation, and also suitable photofatigue resistance without displaying negative photochromism. The SEM images taken from the cellulosic papers impregnated in the CLNPs-20, ELNPs-20, and HLNPs-20 samples indicate high packing and stability of the spherical latex nanoparticles of ELNPs-20 on the surface of cellulose fibers by formation of hydrogen bonding. The ELNPs-20-SPOH sample was selected for formulation of high-security photochromic and fluorescent anticounterfeiting ink and also spraying on cellulosic paper for investigation of high-resolution rewritable photo-patterning. The prepared ink was loaded to stump and applied to the security documents (certificate, money, and passport) for print-marking of different marks with the aid of photochromic and fluorescence labeling. The printed marks display photochromic coloration (colorless to purple) and bright red fluorescence emission, respectively after and under UV illumination (365 nm). Rewritable photo-patterning of the cellulosic papers was carried out by UV illumination under different masks, where the colored patterns were quickly erasable upon visible light or heat. Here, novel and highly efficient strategy was developed for preparation of high-security photochromic and fluorescent anticounterfeiting inks by using of spiropyran derivatives, where the print-marked documents display high level of security by simultaneous reveal of photochromism and fluorescence emission upon UV irradiation. We hope this study help to increase the security of documents and reduce their copy and counterfeiting.
